—The accounts in this chapter demonstrate God’s faithfulness to his promise to Abraham. Esau is here called Edom, a name that kept alive the memory of his selling his birthright for a bowl of soup. Esau remained the same irreverent person who disregarded spiritual things. Those within the covenant are often behind in outward prosperity and honor, while those outside the covenant advance. It may have been a trial to God’s Israel's faith to hear about the pomp and power of the kings of Edom, while they were enslaved in Egypt; but those who look for great things from God must be content to wait; God’s time is the best time. Mount Seir is called the land of their possession. Canaan was at this time only the land of promise. Seir was in the possession of the Edomites. The people of this world have everything in hand and nothing in hope; while the people of God have everything in hope and very little in hand. But, all things considered, it is far better to have Canaan in promise than mount Seir in possession.
